notes: SALMONBERRY (Oneironautics, 1992). Amelie Rocha's most personal game, made right after Fey Faire while grieving a loss she never named: a year in the life of a kid sent to live with a grandmother in the fictional cannery town of Salmonberry, on the mid Oregon coast, across the school year 1963-64. Part adventure (a town, people with stories, a boat that did not come back), part RPG (you grow by living — the aptitudes of a coastal life, a web of bonds, a naturalist's journal — never by fighting). Framed with restraint by the two events everyone from that coast remembers: the Kennedy assassination and the Good Friday tsunami. The grandmother's songs are the Portuguese folk melodies that thread the whole catalog; this is where they came from.

shelf.shelf_condition: played soft · the case corners worn round · a pressed salmonberry leaf still flat in the manual
shelf.prior_owner: Olaf, bought new in 1992 and kept close
shelf.provenance_note: Olaf's note: 'Amelie Rocha's own. She said once it was the only one that was true. I did not ask true about what.'
shelf.cover_blurb: A year in a small town on the coast, a long time ago.  You come to live with your grandmother in Salmonberry, Oregon, in the fall of 1963.  You learn the tides and the people and the woods.  You do not fight anyone.  By June the town knows your name.  From Oneironautics Inc.  One long, quiet game.
back_of_case: · Grow the skills of a coastal life — hands, sea, word, heart, wild, grit.
· A whole town of people to come to know.
· Keep a book of the coast: birds, tides, weather, lore.
· No combat.  No score.  A year, and what you made of it.
'The largest thing we ever tried, and the smallest.'
shelf.in_fiction_dev_note: Amelie Rocha's most personal game, made in 1992 in the two years after Fey Faire, while grieving a loss she never named publicly. It is her grandmother's town, the mid-coast cannery place the family came up in, and the grandmother in the game sings the songs Amelie had already begun putting into everything (the Fey Faire music box was two years old). This is where those melodies came from, admitted once, in a game. The 1992 press did not know what to file it as — too slow for an RPG, too systemic for a memory piece — and it sold accordingly. Amelie called it the only one that was true.
shelf.meta_note: The year is framed by two events the manual never names: a November afternoon the cannery radio goes quiet, and a Good Friday night in March when the water comes up the river. Both are felt through the town, not staged. The traveling show that comes to the cannery lot in the last summer is small and harmless — a Ferris wheel, a fortune tent — twelve years before the Faire that took Amelie's sister. Nobody in 1964 could know that. The game does not say it.

lore_tokens_conditional.salmonberry_keeper: THE KEEPER — the book of the coast near full
lore_tokens_conditional.salmonberry_the_song: THE SONG (true) — you carry the grandmother's melodies out; the catalog's soundtrack begins here
lore_tokens_conditional.salmonberry_the_wave: you helped the night the water came (Good Friday, 1964)
lore_tokens_conditional.salmonberry_faire_seen: the traveling show on the cannery lot, summer 1964 (a 1976 seed)
